question: How can I be sure that my data will be cited properly?
gold_answer: Using someone’s data requires proper citation. As an uploader, add references (papers, websites) that relate to your data. As a user, cite those references together with a stable link (e.g., repository link/DOI) to the data.
title: How can I be sure that my data will be cited properly?
section: General

question: Can I change my email address or affiliation?
gold_answer: Yes. Open **user settings** (click your name after login) and edit your name, email address, and affiliation. The email/affiliation shown on existing data may not update immediately but will be updated eventually.
title: Can I change my email address or affiliation?
section: General

question: I'd like to install NOMAD locally for my group. What should I use?
gold_answer: Use **NOMAD Oasis**, a distribution of NOMAD that can be installed in a local environment to manage your group’s data and integrate with local IT.
title: I'd like to install NOMAD on my local computers to be only used by my group.
section: General

question: Are there any statistics about NOMAD’s data?
gold_answer: Yes. In the search interface, switch to visualization tabs to see statistics (e.g., entries over time or data per code). Metrics count **number of entries/code runs**. Note that large imports (AFLOW, OQMD, Materials Project) are included.
title: Are there any statistics about NOMAD's data?
section: General

question: What steps are needed to publish data and reference it in a paper?
gold_answer:
1) **Upload** your data
2) **Publish** the upload
3) **Create a dataset** and assign a **DOI**
4) **Cite** your data using the DOI or share dataset/entry URLs
title: What steps are necessary to publish data and reference it in a paper or otherwise share it?
section: Upload data, datasets, embargo, and DOIs

question: Do I need to prepare my data before I upload?
gold_answer: Package the files you want to share into a single archive, preferably a **.zip** (or **.tar(.gz)**). Ideally put input/output of each run in its own subdirectory. You may include any additional, relevant files (notes, artifacts).
title: Do I need to prepare my data before I upload?
section: Upload data, datasets, embargo, and DOIs

question: Only some of my files are shown by NOMAD. Where are the others?
gold_answer: NOMAD detects **mainfiles** (recognized main output files) and creates **one entry per mainfile**. Files in the same directory are attached as **auxiliary files** and can be downloaded. If a known main output is not recognized, contact support.
title: Only some of my files are shown by NOMAD, where are the others?
section: Upload data, datasets, embargo, and DOIs

question: Some data is marked 'not processed' or 'unavailable'. What does this mean?
gold_answer: 'Not processed' indicates parsing/processing failed (e.g., evolving file formats). This may change after parser improvements and reprocessing. 'Unavailable' means a quantity doesn’t apply (e.g., symmetry for a single molecule) or algorithms changed; it may change over time.
title: Some of my data is marked as not processed or unavailable, what does this mean?
section: Upload data, datasets, embargo, and DOIs

question: Is my data visible to others?
gold_answer:
• **Unpublished uploads:** visible only to you (and invited collaborators) until published.
• **Embargoed uploads (up to 36 months):** basic metadata is public, but raw files and archives are hidden (except for you and users you share with). You can lift embargo anytime; afterwards data becomes public.
title: Is my data visible to others?
section: Upload data, datasets, embargo, and DOIs

question: How do I cite uploaded data in a paper?
gold_answer: Create a **dataset** (a curated collection of entries) and assign it a **DOI**. Cite the dataset’s DOI so readers can resolve it to NOMAD and inspect/download the data. Datasets can overlap and entries may belong to multiple datasets.
title: How do I cite uploaded data in a paper?
section: Upload data, datasets, embargo, and DOIs

question: How can I create a dataset and assign a DOI?
gold_answer:
1) Ensure data is **uploaded and published**.
2) From **Uploads** or **Your data**, **edit** entries and assign a dataset name (new or existing).
3) Open the **dataset tab** on **Your data** and click the **bookmark/DOI** button to assign a DOI. The DOI is shown on the dataset entry.
title: How can I create a dataset and assign a DOI?
section: Upload data, datasets, embargo, and DOIs

question: Can I change datasets after assigning a DOI?
gold_answer: No. A DOI is a persistent identifier and must always refer to the same data. Therefore, after a DOI is created, you cannot remove data (or whole datasets) from that DOI. DOIs are issued only for datasets containing published (possibly embargoed) data.
title: Can I change datasets after assigning a DOI?
section: Upload data, datasets, embargo, and DOIs

question: How can I share data that is under embargo?
gold_answer: Mark collaborators as **Shared with** when editing entries. They will see embargoed data in **Your data** (toggle **Shared**) and can access it like regular data. You can also share the dataset URL with them.
title: How can I share data that is under embargo?
section: Upload data, datasets, embargo, and DOIs

question: How can I share credit with my co-authors?
gold_answer: Add co-authors via the **Co-authors** field when editing entries. The uploader and listed co-authors form the authors list shown for each entry.
title: How can I share credit with my co-authors?
section: Upload data, datasets, embargo, and DOIs

question: I want to upload data from a code that is not supported. What can I do?
gold_answer: If you know the format, contact support to discuss adding a parser for your code.
title: I want to upload data from a code that is not yet supported.
section: Upload data, datasets, embargo, and DOIs

question: How can I publish data that NOMAD does not (yet) support?
gold_answer: NOMAD needs metadata to publish data. Today, metadata is extracted by **parsers** from supported mainfiles. We’re working on a general way to supply minimal metadata for unsupported formats. Meanwhile, you can include unsupported files alongside a supported **mainfile** (the entry is created from the mainfile; other files in the same directory become auxiliary files).
title: How can I publish data that is not yet supported by NOMAD?
section: Upload data, datasets, embargo, and DOIs

question: How can I share my data via links or URLs?
gold_answer:
• **Public (no embargo):** anyone can open search/result URLs.
• **Embargoed:** add collaborators under **Shared with** so they can access via **Your data → Shared** or search with **Include private**.
• **Datasets:** sharing a dataset link makes entries visible to everyone, but only you/“Shared with” can download files/archives.
• **Uploads page** is only for the owner (not shareable).
title: How can I share my data via links or URLs?
section: Upload data, datasets, embargo, and DOIs

question: My co-authors can’t see my embargoed or unpublished data. Why?
gold_answer: That’s expected. Co-authors are **not** automatically in **Shared with**. You must explicitly add them in the edit dialog; then they’ll see embargoed/unpublished data as shared content.
title: My co-authors can't see my embargoed or unpublished data.
section: Upload data, datasets, embargo, and DOIs

question: What APIs are available?
gold_answer:
Find APIs via the **Analysis** menu (NOMAD or your Oasis):
- **NOMAD API** (proprietary) covering full functionality (also used by the UI and examples)
- **OPTIMADE API** (standardized)
- **Semantic Web/DCAT** interface
title: What APIs are available?
section: API

question: What are the options to access large amounts of NOMAD data?
gold_answer:
Use REST endpoints (e.g., **api/archive**, **api/repo**, **api/raw**) or the **NOMAD Archive client**. For large result sets, use pagination:
- **Page numbers** (limited by Elasticsearch to ~10,000 results)
- **Scroll window** (server returns a scroll id for sequential retrieval)
- **After value** (used by the Archive client; responses include the next after value, e.g., `<upload_id>:<calc_id>`)
You can sometimes pre-compute after values to parallelize requests.
title: What options exist to access large amounts of NOMAD data?
section: API

question: I get a 503 “temporarily not available” error. What should I do?
gold_answer: This typically indicates you’ve hit a **rate limit**. Reduce request rate (fewer requests/second). If you believe the limit is too low for your use case, contact the team to discuss adjustments or exemptions.
title: I get an 503 "temporarily not available" error.
section: API
